item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Oh he's babie

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with the assessment that this doesn’t meet its full potential. You had me at the giant squid puppet show but the dreaming angle honestly just doesn’t work at all. It’s wild, yeah, but it doesn’t come together as a whole or really push the idea to it’s limit. The interviews slowly deflated any tension or mystery and intrigue you set up at the beginning with your description. I think if this has been a giant squid swatting planes full of passengers dreaming about it out of the sky that would’ve been a good way to escalate in a way that followed an internal logic. As it is this doesn’t feel like it follows any kind of internal logic. Stuff just happens and it’s over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the input. I haven’t read this in a while, and I wrote it back when I wasn’t as experienced. I agree in that I can’t think of how to really change the article, but I tried to make it flow better and removed/changed some of the sentences I think didn’t fit in with the squid’s dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed reading it, the… violent enthusiasm of 4664's arrival is pretty funny (he's there to help but fuck anything the person is inside!) and the whole puppet show thing is great; I'd love to see some fanart of the guy!

However, the final interview is a waaaay too convenient massive info dump straight from the horse's, er, kraken's mouth. I'm also not sure the conveniently resistant researcher really had to keep calling it "SCP-4664" to its face if the point was to engender an informative sharing of information, that seems like it would just introduce confusion and such.

But yeah, a full account by the skip itself, in conversational easy-to-understand English, even, explaining everything was a bit of a letdown. I don't know what the best way to end this entry is, but I feel that that's not quite it. Even just ending with the discovery of the ship and the Foundation cataloguing a few pieces of evidence that lets the reader put a story together might be more effective. Dunno!

No vote, but thank you for the visuals of the giant squid puppet show! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original idea for this involved an area of airspace and centipedes. I think this turned out better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was advised to make the squid itself the main SCP instead of the zone it appears. It made the article flow much smoother.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I completely forgot I changed it to gray. I had an idea in my head for the brother but might have decided to nix it. These typos have been fixed and 4664’s big bro has been made yellow once more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4664 violently removes the research tent from the ground, snapping multiple support poles in the process.

Brilliant. Non-Hostile SCP are always a nice departure, and this one manages to be both charming and kind of funny in its ridiculousness. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's weird, cause the last time I read this the puppet was described as yellow everywhere else. If SCP-4664's brother is supposed to be grey, then that's gonna complicate some of the themes I had planned for a little tale series starring him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ You've got a few minor errors.
- I still like it, though.

approximately 25 meters tall

An aquatic animal would generally be described as "long", not "tall".

gradually becoming more opaque until it completely demanifests.

I think you mean "less" opaque — opaque is the opposite of transparent.

(Nothing of note occurs of approximately 2 hours)

Should be "occurs for".

SCP-4664 begins to slam the appendage resembling the vessel against the yellow appendage while making impact sounds.

This puppet is described as "gray" everywhere else.
We hit +50 today! Thank you all for your support!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is far better than the original article I saw (I was going to crit it but it was 11 pm and I had an exam the following morning, so). The grammar is far more refined, the idea is much more explained and understandable, and the concept isn't too unstable either.

+1

item.title: Wholesome
item.content:encoded:
Been a while since i've read something that wasn't a Keter-class skip that risks destroying the fabric of reality.
Squid that makes people happy? Hell yeah, that's a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the start I thought it'd be yet another Lovecraftian monster tricking people to trust them then eating them. I was wrong. Then I thought it would be a misplaced -J with the light sense of humour. I was wrong. The brother is a nice addition, but some more deatails might be missing to reach its full potencial.

+1 nontheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you've pretty much summed up how I feel about it, although I leaned for a no-vote overall. A little rewrite could really make this into something great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this skip was inspired by a Soothouse video? Nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am absolutely baffled at how wild this is on a conceptual level, it's incredibly fun and wonderfully creative!

I am leaving a very hesitant downvote, however; the interview is a huge cop-out, I feel, for conveying backstory. I think you've a creative device to impart this without using direct exposition as a crutch - another "story" or two from the entity could very well cover it for the most part. I think you'd have something much more satisfying if only you allowed readers to meet you halfway, rather than doing the heavy lifting with cliche'd explanation.

-1. Falls shy of reaching its full potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heyo, I remember this from the forums. Have a +1 from me, mainly because there are some great lines in here. I particularly like:

These appendages are made of a cloth-like material, with their appearance being similar to that of a hand puppet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I really meant by this is that, for example, if the person who dreamt of 4664-1 was in a house, this thing just flat out rips the roof off like a kid’s toy. This is why they use a tent for the experiments. They can’t be replacing the site’s roof everytime they want to experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was super fun! The mental image of a giant puppet show squid is conveyed so well, and it's one of the most amazing images ever created. I definitely enjoyed the twist with good squid turning out to be, well, good. The big nitpick is the squid destroying any object between it and the dreamer. Does it try and make sure it doesn't hurt anyone in the process, because this kind of seems to go against its personality. I kind of imagine this big, dumb, friendly squid blundering through buildings, but I don't know how well that's conveyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In all seriousness, having the bad tentacle monster get shoved into the Earth is based off of a bad translation of My Name Is. One of the parts was changed to “I do not give a hell. God has plunged me into the world.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how we only find out what Good Squid believes/knows/remembers about what Bad Squid did and what Papa Squid did about it.

item.title: Re: Hmm....
item.content:encoded: Ment to say demanifest instead of manifest. Fixed.

item.title: Hmm....
item.content:encoded: You seem to contradict yourself, because in the description you wrote that SCP-4664 becomes more opaque and manifests, while in the transcript you say that he becomes translucent and demanifests.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. Fixed those errors, and I’m glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, it was simple, but good. Also, there's a still an SCP-XXX in the interview log and a missing gap just above that, but other than that I enjoyed it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can understand that. I tried to make it a bit unique with the interview revealing a bit about the squid, but everyone has their own opinions. Also, thanks for the compliment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehh.. it’s pretty much a kraken that can speak and cause you to dream and destroy where you are. Novote for now, since it was well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was originally just based off of the pun I used as the title, but it actually turned into something I'm semi-proud of. Huge thanks to Zyn for critiquing this when it was a mess and to the lads in chat for helping me turn a dumb pun into something unique. I'll probably make an article continuing this idea in the future, or maybe I'll just forget about it. Who knows?

Other Articles of Mine
